source_metadata.title_raw: Lan Diep - A-18-018 - February 23, 2018 - San Jose

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Sttite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 9581 t 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Lan Diep, Councilmember 
San Jose District 4 
200 E. Santa Clara Street, 18th Floor 
San Jose, CA 95113 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-018 
Dear Mr. Diep: 
February 23, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the income, gift, and honoraria 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1• Please note that we are only providing advice 
under the provisions of the Act, and not any other bodies oflaw. Also, note that we are not a finder 
of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide 
assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these 
decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May you accept a $2,000 per year stipend for serving on a private foundation's community 
advisory committee? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. You may accept the stipend as income, which must be reported in your statement of 
economic interests. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
You are a Councilmember serving on the San Jose City Council. You have been invited by 
the Knight Foundation (the "Foundation") to participate in a Community Advisory Committee in 
San Jose (the "Committee"). You expect the Committee to consist of approximately 6 members. 
Members are not selected due to the office or title they hold, but because the Foundation believes 
membe~s are leaders in the community. Individuals who serve on the Committee are expected to 
help the Foundation explore local trends, form new partnerships, and determine strategic goals. 
Specifically, in our telephone conversation on February 7, 2018, you noted that as a Committee 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
• 


File No. A-18-018 
Page No. 2 
member, you expect to participate in research, attend meetings with foundation representatives, and 
provide information, advice, and opinions pertaining to groups and organizations within your 
community in an effort to aid the Foundation in achieving its goals. You reported that you expect to 
invest approximately 40 hours or more per year in service to the Committee. In exchange for each 
member's service, the Foundation provides a $2,000 stipend for each year a member serves on the 
Committee. Members may accept the stipend personally, or donate the stipend to a government 
agency or non-profit organization. 
ANALYSIS 
To determine whether you may accept the stipend we must first evaluate whether it would 
be considered honoraria, income, or a gift. When activities for which a person is compensated do 
not fall under the definition of an honorarium, the payment may be considered either a gift or 
income. {Sections 82028 and 82030.) The primary distinction between a gift and income is whether 
the recipient has provided equal consideration in exchange for the payment. If a payment is 
received and the recipient does not provide consideration of equal or greater value to the source of 
the payment, the payment will be considered a gift. Conversely, where the recipient can 
demonstrate that he or she, in fact, provided equal or greater consideration for the payment, the 
payment will be treated as income. (Sodergren Advice Letter, No. I-93-345.) 
Ho11oraria 
Section 89502(a) provides that no elected state official, elected officer of a local government 
agency, or other individual specified in Section 87200 shall accept any honorarium. As a city 
councilmember, you are an elected officer specified in Section 87200. Section 89501(a) generally 
defines an honorarium as "any payment made in consideration for any speech given, article 
published, or attendance at any public or private conference, convention, meeting, social event, 
meal or like gathering." In this case, performing research, providing the Foundation representatives 
with advice and information related to your particular experiences within your community, and 
attending meetings with Foundation representatives to aid the Foundation in decisions related to 
achieving its goals are services that arise to more than merely making a speech, writing an article, 
or attending a meeting or a public or private conference. (Garcia Advice Letter, No. A-01-083, 
Rainey Advice Letter, No. A-98-101, Fessler Advice Letter, No. 1-93-408.) The stipend is 
compensation for the totality of your services over the course of a year, not for attending any one 
meeting, social event, meal or like gathering. (Regulation 18931.3.) Therefore, the $2,000 stipend 
offered in exchange for your services as a Committee member does not meet the definition of an 
honorarium under the Act. 
Gift 
Section 82028 defines a gift as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on the 
recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received," and places the 
burden on the official to show that the official provided consideration of equal or greater value in 
return for the payment. Equal consideration means that each party receives benefits of equal value. 
(In re Burciaga (1975) 2 FPPC Ops. 17.) General guidelines that help determine whether 
consideration is equal or greater in value, include: evidence as to the customary rate of 
compensation for such services, irrespective of official status (Tassi v. Tassi (1958) 160 Cal.App.2d 


File No. A-18-018 
Page No. 3 
680, 690-691), the length of time spent rendering the services, or whether the services are of the 
type not readily available from others. (Matulich Advice Letter, No. 97-304.) The Foundation will 
offer the stipend in exchange for the 40 hours or more per year that you expect to invest as a 
Committee member. Additionally, the Foundation selected you because your experience as a 
community leader uniquely qualifies you to serve as a Committee member to help the Foundation 
meet its strategic goals. Based on the facts, the services you will provide as a Committee member 
will likely amount to consideration of equal or greater value performed in exchange for the $2,000 
stipend. Accordingly, the stipend does not meet the definition of a gift under the Act. 
· I11co111e 
Section 82030 generally defines income as a payment received, including but not limited to 
any salary, wage, advance, gift, reimbursement for expenses or per diem. Income, other than a gift, 
does not include income from any source outside of the jurisdiction and not doing or planning to do 
business in the jurisdiction. (Ibid.) The Foundation does business in San Jose, which means that the 
$2,000 stipend you will receive for your services will be considered income. 
As a public official with full disclosure obligations, you are required by the Act to report 
income and gifts over a certain amount on your statement of economic interests. (See Sections 
82048 and 87200-87210.) Accordingly, you may accept the $2,000 stipend as income, and you 
must disclose the payment in your statement of economic interests.2 
SKP:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
By: 
a K. · uricelli 
Counsel, Legal Division 
2 We also note that should you accept the income, you will be disqualified from taking part in any decisions by 
the San Jose City Council with a reasonably foreseeable material effect on the Foundation.
